entries.text: My special needs daughter is experimenting weird things when she is left alone and bored. Like emptying soaps and shampoos in a wash basin. Her activities make me irritated and anxious but i am also happy that she is learning to get independent

entries.text:
Today was exhausting. I spent most of the day thinking about a promotion that might happen next quarter. I kept comparing myself to my colleagues and wondering why some people seem to advance faster than me.

When my manager praised another team member during the meeting, I felt irritated. I smiled outwardly, but internally I felt jealous. I kept replaying the moment throughout the day.

At home, my daughter wanted to show me a drawing she had made, but I was busy checking emails. I told her I would look at it later. By the time I remembered, she had gone to sleep.

I earned well this month, but strangely I don't feel satisfied.

entries.text:
I had an argument with my spouse today.

The issue was small, but I reacted strongly. I felt she was criticizing me unfairly and immediately became defensive. Later I realized I wasn't really angry about the situation. I was angry because I felt unappreciated.

I noticed that whenever someone questions my decisions, I become uncomfortable. Maybe I depend too much on being seen as competent.

The argument lasted only twenty minutes, but the heaviness stayed with me for the rest of the day.

entries.text:
Work has become increasingly stressful.

I find myself checking stock prices every hour. Even when I am with family, my mind keeps thinking about investments and future financial goals.

I often tell myself that I am doing all this for my family, but sometimes I wonder whether I am simply chasing a moving target.

Every financial milestone gives temporary happiness and then another goal appears.

I don't know what "enough" looks like anymore.

entries.text:
Today I met an old friend.

He earns much less than I do, yet he seemed happier and more relaxed. He spent the evening talking about his children, gardening, and books he enjoys reading.

On the drive home, I felt confused.

For years I believed success would automatically create happiness. Yet many people around me who have achieved a lot seem stressed and dissatisfied.

Maybe I have been measuring success too narrowly.

entries.text:
I caught myself gossiping today.

A colleague made a mistake and I discussed it with another coworker. At the time it felt harmless, but later I realized I was trying to feel superior.

This was uncomfortable to admit.

The mistake had nothing to do with me, yet I spent nearly thirty minutes discussing it.

Perhaps I enjoy feeling better than others more than I realize.

entries.text:
I spent the weekend without social media.

The first day felt strange. I kept reaching for my phone automatically.

By the second day I felt calmer.

I noticed birds in the garden. I played with my daughter without thinking about work. I even sat quietly for twenty minutes without feeling restless.

It made me realize how much mental noise I normally live with.

entries.text:
A difficult situation happened at work today.

Someone took credit for an idea I had contributed to. I felt angry and wanted to confront them immediately.

Instead, I waited until the evening.

When I looked closely, I realized the strongest feeling wasn't anger—it was hurt pride.

I wanted recognition.

The situation still bothers me, but I can see my reaction more clearly now.

entries.text:
My father called today.

We spoke about some old family disagreements. Normally these conversations trigger frustration, but today I mostly listened.

For the first time, I could see that he was carrying his own fears and disappointments.

The conversation wasn't perfect, but it felt softer.

Maybe understanding another person's suffering is more useful than proving who is right.

entries.text:
Something interesting happened this week.

Several things went wrong:

a project was delayed,
an investment declined,
and my travel plans changed.

Yet I noticed less panic than usual.

The problems were still unpleasant, but they didn't completely take over my mind.

A few years ago, I would have spent days worrying.

Perhaps I am slowly learning how to respond instead of react.

entries.text:
Looking back over the last few months, I can see recurring patterns.

I often seek validation through achievement.
I compare myself with others.
I worry about the future more than necessary.
I become defensive when my image is challenged.

But I also notice some changes.

I am becoming more aware of jealousy when it arises.
I recover from anger more quickly.
I spend more time listening.
I feel more gratitude toward my family.

My external life has not changed dramatically.

But something about the way I relate to experiences feels different.

There is still a lot of work to do, but for the first time I feel less interested in becoming "important" and more interested in understanding myself.
